steps.title: Find the base side length from the area
steps.content: Since both pyramids have square bases with area 100 cm², we can find the side length: $s^2 = 100$, so $s = 10$ cm. Both bases are identical, so the difference in total surface area comes entirely from the four triangular lateral faces.

steps.title: Use the Pythagorean theorem to find the slant heights
steps.content:
The slant height of a right square pyramid is the distance from the apex to the midpoint of a base edge. This forms a right triangle with legs: the pyramid height $h$ and the horizontal distance from the base center to the midpoint of an edge, which equals $\frac{s}{2} = 5$ cm.
For pyramid 1 (h = 11 cm):
$$l_1 = \sqrt{11^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{121 + 25} = \sqrt{146} \text{ cm}$$
For pyramid 2 (h = 22 cm):
$$l_2 = \sqrt{22^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{484 + 25} = \sqrt{509} \text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Calculate the lateral surface area difference
steps.content:
Each triangular face has area $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{slant height} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 10 \cdot l = 5l$. The total lateral surface area of each pyramid is $4 \times 5l = 20l$.

The difference in lateral surface area is:
$$20\sqrt{509} - 20\sqrt{146} = 20(\sqrt{509} - \sqrt{146})$$

steps.title: Evaluate numerically to find the closest answer
steps.content:
Computing the approximate values:
$\sqrt{146} \approx 12.08$ and $\sqrt{509} \approx 22.56$

Therefore:
$$20(22.56 - 12.08) = 20 \times 10.48 = 209.6 \text{ cm}^2$$

This is closest to choice A — A is correct.
